stocks.note: Coal producer. Acquired by Alpha Natural Resources (ANR) completed June 1, 2011: each MEE share converted to $10.00 cash + 1.025 ANR shares, deal valued ~$69.33/sh at announcement (Jan 2011). Year-end closes 2005-2010 from 1stock1 (37.87, 23.23, 35.75, 13.79, 42.01, 53.65). All-time high ~$94 in June 2008 (commodity boom); collapsed to ~$13.79 year-end 2008 in the crash, then recovered. 2010 also hit by the April 2010 Upper Big Branch disaster mid-year dip. Earliest years (2002-2004) approximate: Massey was spun off from Fluor in late 2000 and traded in the low-teens-to-$30s range as coal prices rose. Treated as cash_buyout since a cash component plus acquirer stock was paid; classified by primary corporate fate (absorbed into ANR, ticker retired).

Natural-gas pipeline / energy company. Acquired by Kinder Morgan (KMI), completed May 24-25, 2012: each EP share converted to $14.65 cash + 0.4187 KMI shares + 0.640 of a 5-year KMI warrant (strike $40). Deal valued ~$26.87/sh at Oct 2011 announcement. Year-end closes from 1stock1: 2001 $44.61, then a severe crash to $6.96 at year-end 2002 in the energy-merchant/Enron-era bust (Williams, Dynegy, El Paso all collapsed), slow recovery to ~$17 by 2007, dip to $7.83 in the 2008 crisis, then a strong run to $26.57 by end-2011 on the takeover. share_ratio is the KMI common-stock ratio; the additional 0.640 warrant per share is noted separately. Cash_buyout because a large cash component was paid alongside acquirer stock; ticker EP retired.

Local/landline telecom spun off from Sprint Nextel on May 17, 2006 (one EQ share per 20 Sprint shares); first NYSE trade May 18, 2006 under ticker EQ. Acquired by CenturyTel (CTL, later renamed CenturyLink) in an all-stock deal completed July 1, 2009: each EQ share converted to 1.37 CTL shares. At the Oct 24, 2008 announcement the exchange ratio implied ~$40.42/EQ-share (a ~36% premium). Anchors are reconstructed estimates: EQ debuted in the high-$40s, held in the $45-55 range through 2006-2007 as a high-dividend telecom, declined to the mid-$30s in the 2008 market crash, and recovered toward ~$45-50 into the mid-2009 close as the CTL deal completed. Exact daily history is sparse online (the EQ ticker now maps to unrelated companies), hence medium confidence; magnitudes are consistent with the $30-60 trading range and the ~1.37x/$40-50 takeout. Pure stock swap, no cash; ticker EQ retired.

Office-supply retailer. The former Boise Cascade Corp renamed itself OfficeMax in 2004 after selling its paper/forest assets and keeping the OfficeMax retail brand (ticker OMX). Merged with Office Depot (ODP), completed November 5, 2013: each OMX share converted to 2.69 ODP shares (all-stock, ~$13-15 implied value). Year-end closes from 1stock1: 2003 $32.86 down to $25.36 in 2005, spike-free peak ~$49.65 at end-2006, then a steep decline to $20.66 (2007), $7.64 (2008 crisis), partial recoveries and another trough at $4.54 end-2011, recovering to $9.76 (2012) and ~$14.23 at the merger. Never traded near $50+ beyond the 2006 year-end; no order-of-magnitude spikes. Pure stock swap, no cash; ticker OMX retired.

Atlanta-based bank. Merged with BB&T (a 'merger of equals') to form Truist Financial (TFC), completed December 6, 2019: each STI share converted to 1.295 Truist (TFC) shares, worth ~$68 at close. Year-end closes from 1stock1: traded in the $56-84 range 2001-2006 (peak $84.45 end-2006), fell to $62.49 (2007) and $29.54 (2008) in the financial crisis, with the intraday/quarter low around ~$6-7 in early March 2009 (added as a 2009-03-31 anchor) before recovering to $20.29 by year-end. Steady recovery through the 2010s to the $50-65 range. Never traded anywhere near $577 (prior corruption); magnitudes are consistent. Pure stock swap, no cash; ticker STI retired and replaced by TFC.
